title: Watching Shapes Change Through the Network
concept: Tracking height, width, and channels step by step makes a CNN architecture much easier to understand: convolutions often change channels, pooling shrinks space, ReLU keeps shape, and the head converts the final feature map into logits.

exercises.prompt: Recap: In a feature-map label like 16x16x8, what does the 8 usually represent?
exercises.explain: Right — the last number is the channel count.

options.text: The image file size in megabytes

cards.title: The shape trail tells the story
cards.body: When a CNN feels confusing, shape tracking is a great rescue tool. If you know what happened to height, width, and channels at each step, the model becomes much easier to read.

cards.title: What each dimension is doing
cards.body: A label like H x W x C means height, width, and channels. In many beginner examples, a padded convolution keeps H and W the same while changing C. ReLU usually keeps the shape unchanged. Pooling often shrinks height and width. GlobalAvgPool turns an entire H x W x C map into just C numbers.

cards.title: Like tracking packages through a warehouse
cards.body: Imagine packages moving down a line. Some stations repack them into smaller boxes, some add more labeled bins, and one final station turns the whole shipment into a short report. CNN layers do something similar to shapes.

cards.title: Track one network end to end
cards.body: Using same-padding convolutions, a toy network could go 32x32x3 -> Conv(8) -> 32x32x8 -> ReLU -> 32x32x8 -> MaxPool -> 16x16x8 -> Conv(16) -> 16x16x16 -> ReLU -> 16x16x16 -> MaxPool -> 8x8x16 -> GlobalAvgPool -> 16 -> Dense -> 4 logits. Notice the pattern: convolution grows channels, ReLU keeps the shape, pooling shrinks space, and the head converts the last feature stack into class scores.

cards.title: More channels does not mean a bigger image
cards.body: A common mix-up is thinking that more channels means the image got physically larger. It did not. Channels mean more feature types stacked at each location, not more height or width.

cards.title: Spatial size vs. channel depth
cards.body: These two kinds of shape changes are different jobs.

left.text:
32x32 -> 16x16 -> 8x8
Pooling or stride shrinks space
Less exact location detail

right.text:
3 -> 8 -> 16
Convolutions can grow feature types
Richer detectors at each spot

exercises.prompt: If a 16x16x8 feature map passes through a pooling layer that halves height and width, what is the new shape?
exercises.explain: Correct — pooling halves the spatial size here but keeps the same 8 channels.

exercises.prompt: True or false: ReLU usually changes the number of channels in a feature map.
exercises.explain: False — ReLU changes values, not the usual shape label.
Yes — global average pooling compresses each channel to one number, so 8x8x16 becomes 16 numbers.
exercises.sentence: Global average pooling turns an 8x8x16 map into ___ numbers.

exercises.prompt: Which step most naturally changes a feature stack from 16x16x8 to 16x16x16 while keeping the same spatial size?
exercises.explain: Exactly — a convolution that outputs 16 channels can do that while keeping the same height and width if padding is used.

options.text: A convolution layer that outputs 16 channels with same padding
Deleting half the image with pooling

exercises.prompt: Which shape trace is the correct reading of this toy CNN: 32x32x3 -> Conv(8) -> ReLU -> Pool -> Conv(16) -> ReLU -> Pool -> GlobalAvgPool -> Dense -> 4 logits, assuming the convolutions keep spatial size the same?
exercises.explain: Exactly. That trace respects what each layer usually changes: convolution grows channels, ReLU keeps shape, pooling halves space, and global pooling squeezes the final channels to one number each before the dense output.
